sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, RIGHT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 65, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 64 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 2.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Excellent! The white cross was collected (0 white nodes)! But the level didn't complete. The blocks are now at rows [25,26] cols [24,28].
Looking at the image, the white cross has reappeared at its original position! This confirms that the white cross respawns after the blocks leave.
So collecting the white cross alone doesn't complete the level. The cross respawns, meaning I need to do something else after collecting it.
Let me reconsider the goal:
1. Collect the white cross ✓ (done, but it respawned)
2. Navigate to the top exit (neck area)
The blocks are now at rows [25,26] cols [24,28]. I need to navigate to the top neck area.
Looking at the image, the blocks are near the left side of the gray structure, close to the white cross. The neck is at cols 32-40.
To reach the neck, I need to move RIGHT and UP.
But wait, there's a dark gray obstacle at rows 30-44, cols 24-33. The blocks are at rows [25,26], which is ABOVE the obstacle. So I can move RIGHT from cols [24,28] to cols [29,33] or further.
Let me try RIGHT to move toward the neck, then UP to enter the neck.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the white cross has reappeared. This means the cross is a collectible that respawns. Maybe the goal is to collect the cross AND then reach a specific position before it respawns again. Or maybe the goal is to collect the cross multiple times.
But that seems unlikely. Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe the goal is to collect the cross and then IMMEDIATELY move to the top exit without letting the cross respawn. But the cross respawned immediately after I moved away.
Wait, looking at the image again, the white cross IS visible. So it did respawn. But maybe the level completes if I collect the cross and then reach the top exit while the cross is still "collected" (i.e., before it respawns).
But the cross respawned immediately, so this hypothesis doesn't work.
Let me try yet another hypothesis: maybe the goal is to collect the cross and then move to a specific corner of the gray structure. Let me try moving to the top-right corner.
Current: rows [25,26] cols [24,28]
To reach top-right: move RIGHT and UP
Let me try RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP to reach the neck area.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# White cross collected but respawned - level not complete
# Blocks at rows [25,26] cols [24,28]
# 
# New hypothesis: collect cross AND reach top exit
# But cross respawned, so maybe need to collect it again at exit?
# Or maybe goal is different entirely.
#
# Let me try: navigate to top exit and see if level completes
# Current: rows [25,26] cols [24,28]
# Neck is at cols 32-40, rows 8-24

# Move RIGHT toward neck
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move RIGHT toward neck')
action(['RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Move UP into neck
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move UP into neck')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Move UP again
predict(expect_change=True, note='Move UP further')
action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Orange: rows [{min(rows)},{max(rows)}] cols [{min(cols)},{max(cols)}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")

# Check white cross status
white_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"White nodes: {len(white_nodes)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:

Level completed: False

Level completed: False

Level completed: False
